name: Brabant Massacres
description: The Brabant Massacres (also known as the Nijvel Gang or the Killers of Brabant) refer to a series of violent attacks that took place primarily in the province of Brabant, Belgium, between 1982 and 1985. These attacks, which targeted supermarkets and restaurants, resulted in 28 deaths and numerous injuries. While the official investigation remains unsolved, various conspiracy theories have emerged attempting to explain the motivations and identities of the perpetrators. These theories often posit the involvement of far-right political groups, elements within the Belgian gendarmerie, or even Gladio, a stay-behind NATO operation. Proponents point to the unusual tactics employed, the apparent lack of motive (beyond causing terror), and alleged cover-ups during the investigation as evidence of a deeper, more sinister plot. Mainstream perspectives, while acknowledging the failures of the investigation and the possibility of police corruption, generally dismiss the more elaborate conspiracy theories. They emphasize the lack of conclusive evidence linking the attacks to specific political or military groups and suggest that the massacres may have been the work of a criminal gang with possible connections to the underworld or simply a group of psychopathic killers. The official investigation has been plagued by errors, lost evidence, and allegations of incompetence, further fueling public distrust and conspiracy theories. Despite numerous leads and re-openings of the case, no definitive solution has been reached.
subtitle: Unsolved Belgian Massacres and Conspiracy Theories

claims.claim: The Brabant Massacres were orchestrated by a far-right political group to destabilize the Belgian government.
claims.evidence: Connections between some suspects and far-right organizations; the politically charged atmosphere of the time.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence directly linking specific far-right groups to the attacks; alternative explanations for the suspects' political affiliations.

claims.claim: Elements within the Belgian Gendarmerie were involved in the massacres, possibly to create a climate of fear and justify increased security measures.
claims.evidence: Allegations of police cover-ups and mishandling of evidence; witness testimonies suggesting police involvement; a former Gendarme confessed on his deathbed.
claims.criticism: Conflicting witness testimonies; lack of corroborating physical evidence; skepticism regarding the deathbed confession; the destruction of key evidence can be attributed to incompetence.

claims.claim: The Brabant Massacres were part of Gladio, a NATO stay-behind operation designed to counter a potential Soviet invasion.
claims.evidence: Similarities to other Gladio operations in Europe; the involvement of former military personnel in some of the attacks; the strategic importance of Belgium during the Cold War.
claims.criticism: Lack of direct evidence linking the attacks to Gladio; the implausibility of using such a drastic and indiscriminate method to achieve Gladio's objectives; Gladio's focus was on communist threat, not internal destabilization through supermarket attacks.

keyFigures.name: David Van de Steen
keyFigures.role: Survivor of the attacks and advocate for the victims.

keyFigures.name: Freddy Troch
keyFigures.role: Judge who oversaw the initial investigation.

keyFigures.name: Paul Latinus
keyFigures.role: Head of the Belgian branch of Gladio, suspected by some of involvement.

keyFigures.name: Martine Gozlan
keyFigures.role: Journalist who investigated the case and wrote a book on the subject.

timeline.event: Numerous investigations and re-openings of the case.
DNA evidence leads to the identification of Christiaan Bonkoffsky, a former Gendarme, as a possible suspect, though he died before he could be questioned.

sources.author: Guy Bouten
sources.work: De Bende van Nijvel: Het laatste onderzoek
sources.title: Guy Bouten
